0: <title>Cradle-to-gate LCA of poultry production system in a developing country - The case of Nigeria </title>

* Ewemoje T. A. Omotosho O., Abimbola O.P. Agricultural and Environmental Engineering Department, Faculty of Technology, University of Ibadan, Ibadan. *Author for correspondence ta.ewemoje@mail.ui.edu.ng,tayo_ewemoje@yahoo.co.uk)
Received January 2013; accepted in revised form March 2013
1: The study assessed environmental impacts loads of production processes from hatchery to point- of-lay using Life Cycle Assessment (LCA). The functional unit (FU) used was one point-of-lay bird. Results showed total contributions to global warming were 9.708kg of CO2 equivalent, 11.34 kg of CH4 equivalent and 0.2kg of NO2 equivalent. Contributions to acidification were 2.713 X 104kg of SO2 equivalent, 1.948 X 10-3 kg of NH3 equivalent, 2.167 X 10 kg of NO equivalent and energy consumption was 59.79KJ per bird. Hatchery processes, waste disposal, water pumping and feed production respectively accounted for 543.2J, 6J, I6J and 46.5J of energy per bird. Scenario analysis based on a typical developing country with incessant power outages indicated that using or relying on 100% public power reduces CO2, NO and SO2 equivalents by 95.6%, 10.6% and 15.6%, while a more realistic 50-50% diesel-public power mix reduces CO2, NOx and SO2 equivalents by 47.8%, 5.2% and 21.5% respectively. Frequent removal of litter and manure from poultry houses has been advocated to reduce ammonia emissions, but the cost of cleanout and bedding replacement makes this not to be a viable option in this study. A more feasible approach in reducing environmental impacts recommended is to control litter moisture by reducing water spillages, as more moist litter implies increased potential for ammonia emissions.
Keywords: Developing country, Environmental loads, Greenhouse gases, Life-cycle-assessment, Poultry production.

3: An overview of livestock's impact on climate change suggests that the global contribution of anthropogenic green house gas (GHG) emissions from the livestock sector is approximately 18-20% of global anthropogenic GHG emissions (Monteny et al. 2006, Pitesky et al. 2009). In comparison of the agricultural sector to global fossil fuel burning which accounted for approximately 13% of the global anthropogenic GHG emissions (Steinfeld et al. 2006), the environmental load is a cause for concern.

The worrying situation stems from the fact that approximately 80% of the world population lives in developing countries and this population emits approximately two- thirds of all anthropogenic agricultural GHG emissions (Smith et al. 2007). Due to awareness of environmental problem of global warming and the societal concern over environmental issues, the animal industry has recognized the need to solve environmental problems in this industry by identifying and quantifying environmental loads or impacts such production system

contributes to the environment (Ogino et al. 2007). The source of the three primary GHGs (CO2, CH4 and N20) considered to be of concern in environmental pollution has been linked to agriculture, which can also be a sink for CO2 through carbon sequestration into biomass products and soil organic matter (Johnson, et al. 2007).

A method developed to assess environmental impacts and which has been standardized internationally is the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA). It is a type of integrated environmental impact assessment (Ogino et al. 2008). The LCA application has been extended to the identification of environmental improvement opportunities; strategic planning and development; product and process optimization, design, environmental reporting and marketing (Hospido et al. 2003).

The dynamics of global climate change includes increased sea level, changes in rainfall distribution and storm intensity; current strategies for coping with this phenomenon is by reducing fossil fuel combustion, curbing GHGs emissions and increasing carbon sequestration (Johnson et al. 2007). Since environmental protection practice has become inevitable due to the wide felt effects of global warming, it is therefore important to assess the various effects of activities in a poultry production. Thus, the aim of this study was to investigate the LCA of processes from hatchery to point-of-lay stage using deep litter system of poultry management in Nigeria, a typical developing country. The study also investigated the environmental impacts of the sole use of public power supply against 50-50% diesel-public power supply scenario. The study scope was limited to the assessment of the environmental impact of GHGs, as well as other wastes generated from hatchery production processes to point-of-lay birds in
a deep litter management system with Nera Black Breed of layers.

6: The functional unit of this LCA is defined as one chicken at point-of-lay. According to Ogino et al. 2007, "functional unit is a reference to which all other materials and associated environmental loads in the LCA are related". The cradle-to-gate system analyzed the life cycle of incubated egg for 21 days through 18-week period up to point- of-lay (Figure 1).
7: An inventory of all the resources used within the system boundary was taken from incubation stage to point-of-lay stage. Calculated total feed input for the 30,000 birds was 248,469.9 kg (Tables 1 and 2) and total water usage was 1,920 m³ 3 All the energy inputs into the system are shown in Table 3. Energy and emissions involved in transportation were exempted from the calculations. Table 3 also showed some of the computed emission coefficients while the total energy from the combustion of charcoal for brooding purpose was computed using a calorific value of 29,600 kJ/kg.

Environmental loads associated with incubated eggs to point-of-lay production were shown in Table 4. The table analyzes the emission outputs from the various activities involved such as feed production, water pumping and supply, power generation and the waste management. Odour emission from waste was calculated using Gay et al. 2002 coefficient chart. The US EPA Greenhouse Gas Equivalence Calculator was used to compute the CO2 equivalent in feed production while diesel fuel consumption chart was used to compute the average fuel consumption by the generators (Fuglestvedt et al. 2009). Pollutants emitted by feed production stage were obtained by;
CO2, CH4, N2O, NH3, NOx, SOx

14: The LCA applied the use of 100% purchased or public power supply and a scenario of 50-50% use of diesel-public power supply. However, many poultry farmers in developing countries depend solely on 100% diesel generators for power supply. Hence, this study investigated if there would be a significant difference in level of the environmental pollution with the 50-50% scenario. Environmental loads associated with the use of 8,663MW of electricity from public power source calculated using the coefficients for thermal and hydroelectric processes were obtained using the current installed power generation capacity in Nigeria while emissions from diesel generators was obtained using the diesel consumption chart and diesel combustion CO2 coefficient. Total electricity usage for the period was estimated at 1,820kWh.
15: <header>RESULTS AND DISCUSSION </header>

Environmental Impacts of the Egg-Point of Lay System: The analysis showed that on the average, solid waste accounted for about 68%, liquid waste about 23% while gaseous waste accounted for about 9% (Figure 2). This is indicative of the high volume and strength of solid waste in poultry which is dependent on age, animal physiological state and many other conditions. Liquid wastes have a relatively lower volume and strength which may also vary depending on the
management practice, drugs and chemical usage.

The hatchery process had an average gaseous output of about 10%, liquid output of 41% while solid waste accounted for about 49% (Figure 3), this implied that adequate attention should be given to the treatment of both solid waste and liquid effluent. Wash water had high strength due to the use of chemicals and disinfectants like formalin, potassium permanganate and a high volume due to the need for improved sanitary conditions within the farm. The solid waste consisted of dead-in-shell eggs, egg shells, cartons, and empty chemical containers.

Investigation of the contributions of each production process to global warming potential i.e. hatchery process, energy generation, feed production, waste disposal, water supply and biological activities is shown in Table 5. The total CO2 equivalent accounted for 8.76% of the total emissions, CH4 emission accounted for 90.64% of the total emission while N2O was 0.70%. Feed production accounted for 2.5% in form of CO2, energy production from diesel generator accounted for the highest amount of CO2 of 73.96% while water pumping was the lowest with 1.13% of CO2 equivalent. While CO2 is seen as a major contributor to environmental loads, black carbon (sooth) is also a concern to global warming. Methane (CH4) is produced by the microbial degradation of soluble lipids, carbohydrates, organic acids, proteins, and other organic components. The H2S emitted by the waste from the poultry house was 1.386 X 10 Kg per functional unit. It has been reported that CH4 contributes 9-20% of the total global warming potential (Sommer and Moller 2000).

The power consumption analysis was also investigated to determine if actually there is an energy balance and also to verify the effect of the current practice in Nigeria, a typical developing country on the environment. The result showed that the feed production process consumed about 46.5 J of energy per bird, while pumping of water accounted for about 16J per bird and the waste disposal was about 6J per bird. The power involved in waste disposal was calculated on a man-hours per day basis taking into account the average power rating of human labour as 0.075kw, this value was then multiplied by the total time expended in waste disposal. The hatchery process had a
value of about 543.2J per bird of energy input which was the highest. The result showed that if total energy consumption is to be minimized the hatchery processes has to be focused on.

Ammonia and its chemical constituents are important components responsible for acidification in addition to Sulfur compounds (SOx), Nitrogen oxides, and volatile organic components (Koerkamp 1994, Koerkamp et al. 1998). This implies that these compounds are also very crucial to the acidification potential of the system on the environment. The result from table 6 showed that biological process stage on the system has the highest NH3. and SO2 output

coefficient of 119kg and 5.43kg; hatchery process has the highest NO value of 7.21kg. Energy production stage had the lowest SO2 and NH3 values while water production had the lowest value of NOx. This showed that adequate attention needs to be given to the

waste disposal of hatchery waste and treatment methods in other to reduce the strength of the waste before final disposal, biological treatments like use of composting pits, and septic tanks may be adopted.

20: <header>RESULTS AND DISCUSSION </header>

Environmental Impacts of the Egg-Point of Lay System: The analysis showed that on the average, solid waste accounted for about 68%, liquid waste about 23% while gaseous waste accounted for about 9% (Figure 2). This is indicative of the high volume and strength of solid waste in poultry which is dependent on age, animal physiological state and many other conditions. Liquid wastes have a relatively lower volume and strength which may also vary depending on the
management practice, drugs and chemical usage.

The hatchery process had an average gaseous output of about 10%, liquid output of 41% while solid waste accounted for about 49% (Figure 3), this implied that adequate attention should be given to the treatment of both solid waste and liquid effluent. Wash water had high strength due to the use of chemicals and disinfectants like formalin, potassium permanganate and a high volume due to the need for improved sanitary conditions within the farm. The solid waste consisted of dead-in-shell eggs, egg shells, cartons, and empty chemical containers.

Investigation of the contributions of each production process to global warming potential i.e. hatchery process, energy generation, feed production, waste disposal, water supply and biological activities is shown in Table 5. The total CO2 equivalent accounted for 8.76% of the total emissions, CH4 emission accounted for 90.64% of the total emission while N2O was 0.70%. Feed production accounted for 2.5% in form of CO2, energy production from diesel generator accounted for the highest amount of CO2 of 73.96% while water pumping was the lowest with 1.13% of CO2 equivalent. While CO2 is seen as a major contributor to environmental loads, black carbon (sooth) is also a concern to global warming. Methane (CH4) is produced by the microbial degradation of soluble lipids, carbohydrates, organic acids, proteins, and other organic components. The H2S emitted by the waste from the poultry house was 1.386 X 10 Kg per functional unit. It has been reported that CH4 contributes 9-20% of the total global warming potential (Sommer and Moller 2000).

The power consumption analysis was also investigated to determine if actually there is an energy balance and also to verify the effect of the current practice in Nigeria, a typical developing country on the environment. The result showed that the feed production process consumed about 46.5 J of energy per bird, while pumping of water accounted for about 16J per bird and the waste disposal was about 6J per bird. The power involved in waste disposal was calculated on a man-hours per day basis taking into account the average power rating of human labour as 0.075kw, this value was then multiplied by the total time expended in waste disposal. The hatchery process had a
value of about 543.2J per bird of energy input which was the highest. The result showed that if total energy consumption is to be minimized the hatchery processes has to be focused on.

Ammonia and its chemical constituents are important components responsible for acidification in addition to Sulfur compounds (SOx), Nitrogen oxides, and volatile organic components (Koerkamp 1994, Koerkamp et al. 1998). This implies that these compounds are also very crucial to the acidification potential of the system on the environment. The result from table 6 showed that biological process stage on the system has the highest NH3. and SO2 output

coefficient of 119kg and 5.43kg; hatchery process has the highest NO value of 7.21kg. Energy production stage had the lowest SO2 and NH3 values while water production had the lowest value of NOx. This showed that adequate attention needs to be given to the

waste disposal of hatchery waste and treatment methods in other to reduce the strength of the waste before final disposal, biological treatments like use of composting pits, and septic tanks may be adopted.

21: Results obtained from the two scenarios showed significant reduction in each of the environmental load coefficients in the system. Scenario analysis based on a typical developing country such as Nigeria with unstable power supply showed that using 100% public power supply will reduce the CO2, NO and SO2 equivalents by 95.6%, 10.6% and 15.6%, while using a more realistic 50-50% diesel-public power supply will reduce CO2, NO and SO2 equivalents by 47.8%, 5.2% and 21.5% respectively. It can therefore be deduced from the results in Figure 4 and Table 7 that the use of diesel generator as power source is not environmentally friendly as this emits a relatively large volume of greenhouse gasses which may lead to an increase in global warming and acidification potentials as well as noise pollution when compared to the use of public power supply.
Comparing the 100% use of diesel engine generator to the scenario of the 50-50% use of diesel generator and public power supply,

results in Table 7 showed that the contribution of livestock hatchery production up to the point of lay birds to global warming and acidification potential was reduced by approximately 50% in all the environmental impacts indices assessed in the study.

23: To reduce the environmental impacts, frequent removal of litter and manure from poultry houses would reduce ammonia emissions, but due to the cost of cleanout and bedding replacement, this may not be a viable option. A more feasible approach would be to control litter moisture by reducing water spillages, as more moist litter implies increased potential for ammonia emissions. Ferguson et al. 1998 confirmed the relationship between higher litter moisture and increased litter ammonia. Ventilation is critical to maintaining proper litter moisture.

Water usage should be as economical as possible as a large chunk of energy usage was on water pumping, and where possible, reuse of water should be encouraged. Also, use of novel pest repellant composition is encouraged against the use of toxic organophosphates because novel pest repellant composition is more environmentally friendly. Novel pest repellant composition contains gallic juice, water, neem oil, organo-polysiloxane and pepper wax.

Compost method of waste (manure) treatment should be employed as it reduces the presence of pathogens and makes the organic matter more biodegradable. The method also suppresses or eliminates the possibility of eutrophication due to surface run-off which may contain phosphate and nitrogenous compounds. The use of septic tanks could also be used as a form of anaerobic treatment before waste is disposed. This reduces odour emissions from waste treatment.

Other environmentally friendly alternative power sources such as wind, hydroelectricity and solar should be employed where possible in production process in order to reduce reliance on fossil fuel (petrol and diesel) generators.
